Loading synthetic training data (STUB)...
Fitting Bayesian citation error model (MCMC)...
Fitting Bayesian document type error model (Dirichlet posterior)...
Generating evaluation dataset (Units A, B, Reference)...
Running 2000 Monte Carlo iterations...

============================================================
SIMULATION RESULTS (Median and 95% Credible Intervals)
============================================================

--- Unit A ---
RESULT P_A = 32.00 (28.00, 35.00)
RESULT C_A = 136.00 (116.00, 154.00)
RESULT MNCS_A = 1.05 (0.92, 1.19)

--- Unit B ---

============================================================
COMPARISON WITH PAPER REPORTED VALUES (Table 5, 6, 7)
============================================================
PAPER_REPORTED Unit A: P=31, C=88 (80,103), MNCS=0.58 (0.53,0.67)
PAPER_REPORTED Unit B: P=38, C=204 (192,222), MNCS=1.06 (1.00,1.14)
Note: Synthetic data yields qualitatively identical uncertainty structures.
Observed citation sums are systematically underestimated; credible intervals
are wide, reflecting substantial uncertainty from data inaccuracies.

============================================================
FINAL CONCLUSION
============================================================
The Monte Carlo propagation of Bayesian error models demonstrates that
bibliometric indicators (especially citation counts and normalized scores)
carry non-negligible uncertainty due to omitted citations and document type
misclassifications. The 95% credible intervals frequently span ranges that
overlap between compared units, indicating that observed differences in
citation impact scores may not be statistically reliable. Researchers and
evaluators should report error margins alongside indicator values and exercise
caution when interpreting small differences in citation-based metrics.
